title: The Secret War Of Lisa Simpson

jokes.text: D'oh! [Screams]
jokes.setup: Opening couch gag physical comedy sequence
jokes.punchline: Homer's signature exclamation after presumed mishap
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer catchphrase with perfect character integration. Economy is excellent but lacks originality as it's overused by this point.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Oh, can't anybody in this town take the law into their own hands?
jokes.setup: Police chief checking messages, presumably about crime
jokes.punchline: Chief wanting citizens to become vigilantes instead of calling police
jokes.explanation: Perfect Wiggum character moment - incompetent cop wanting to avoid work. Well-structured irony.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: You've reached the Springfield Police Department. If you have an emergency, hang up and call 911. If you'd like to report a crime, press 1. If you'd like to report a crime in progress, press 2. If you'd like to become a vigilante instead of wasting our time, press 3.

jokes.text: While Johnny Welfare plays acid rock on a stolen guitar... his old lady has a better idea... She's got the 'munchies' for a California cheeseburger
jokes.setup: Anti-drug educational film narration
jokes.punchline: Absurdly specific and dated drug stereotypes culminating in regional food craving
jokes.explanation: Excellent satire of overwrought anti-drug films. The specificity of 'California cheeseburger' elevates it.

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.line: This is your brain on drugs.

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.line: Any questions?

jokes.text: So, the next time you're walking on the beach... enjoying an hourglass or making cheap, low-grade windshields... think where we'd be without sand
jokes.setup: Educational film about sand's importance
jokes.punchline: Increasingly absurd and specific uses for sand, especially 'enjoying an hourglass'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist writing. 'Enjoying an hourglass' as a beach activity is wonderfully ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Educational Film Narrator
dialogue.line: Sand has many uses. It is used in the production of glass, in the manufacture of computer chips, and as an essential component of concrete. But sand has even more practical applications.

dialogue.speaker: Educational Film Narrator
dialogue.line: Sand can be used for sandpapering, for filling sandbags, and for creating sandhills. Sand is useful for making sandwiches, for sanding one's feet, and for enjoying an hourglass.

dialogue.speaker: Educational Film Narrator
dialogue.line: Sand can even be used for traction on icy roads, for spreading on beaches, and for throwing in one's eyes during an argument.

jokes.text: Uh, Miss Hoover, movies are a nice break... but couldn't we be doing something a little more challenging? Probably.
jokes.setup: Lisa requests more challenging curriculum
jokes.punchline: Teacher's utterly indifferent and honest response
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and character work. Miss Hoover's apathy is beautifully captured in one word.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Miss Hoover, I'd like to request a more challenging curriculum.

dialogue.speaker: Miss Hoover
dialogue.line: Lisa, I'd love to help, but I'm not a miracle worker. I'm barely getting through the day myself. Besides, standardized tests don't measure critical thinking—they measure how well you can take standardized tests.

jokes.text: I have no objection to man walking on the moon
jokes.setup: 1950s documentary about moon exploration
jokes.punchline: Politician's hilariously underwhelming endorsement of space exploration
jokes.explanation: Great period satire. The diplomatic understatement for something so ambitious is perfectly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Adlai Stevenson
dialogue.line: Gentlemen, I assure you that space exploration will not interfere with my nap time.

jokes.text: Slow down, tubby. You're not on the moon yet
jokes.setup: Narrator explaining reduced moon gravity
jokes.punchline: Condescending fat joke directed at viewer
jokes.explanation: Good period filmstrip satire but relies on cheap fat joke. Economy saves it.

dialogue.speaker: Educational Film Narrator
dialogue.line: Due to the moon's reduced gravity, you would weigh only one-sixth of what you do on Earth. Of course, you would still look like a blimp.

jokes.text: Maybe she drove to the moon
jokes.setup: Students notice Miss Hoover's car is gone
jokes.punchline: Child's literal interpretation of moon travel possibility
jokes.explanation: Sweet child logic callback to moon film. Good character voice for anonymous student.

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Miss Hoover's car is gone!

dialogue.speaker: Ralph Wiggum
dialogue.line: Maybe she went to the moon.

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: No one can go to the moon.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph Wiggum
dialogue.line: They said you can go to the moon, so you can go to the moon.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph Wiggum
dialogue.line: I'm gonna go to the moon!

jokes.text: Of course we could make things more challenging, Lisa... but then the stupider students would be in here complaining... furrowing their brows in a vain attempt to understand the situation
jokes.setup: Lisa complaining about lack of educational challenge
jokes.punchline: Teacher's brutally honest assessment of her other students' limitations
jokes.explanation: Excellent teacher cynicism. 'Furrowing their brows in a vain attempt' is wonderfully specific and mean.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa Simpson
dialogue.line: I'm not being challenged in school. The curriculum is way too easy for me.

dialogue.speaker: Miss Hoover
dialogue.line: Lisa, you're the only student in this class who can read. Ralph over there thinks the sun is a hot monster. Bart believes history ended in 1987. And don't even get me started on what Milhouse thinks photosynthesis is.

jokes.text: Uh, there's Chiquita. That's Del Monte. There's a Banan Co. Gorilla's Choice
jokes.setup: Wiggum showing important police radio covered in banana stickers
jokes.punchline: Detailed catalog of banana brands as if it's police procedure
jokes.explanation: Good Wiggum moment. The made-up 'Gorilla's Choice' brand elevates the mundane catalog.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Here's our important police radio. As you can see, it's covered in banana stickers.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: We've got Chiquita, Dole, Del Monte, Señor Bananas...

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Each one catalogued and filed according to proper police procedure.

jokes.text: Oh, I won't be getting up soon
jokes.setup: Wiggum sits down heavily in chair during tour
jokes.punchline: Honest admission he's too lazy to continue tour
jokes.explanation: Perfect Wiggum character moment. Simple but effective economy.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm too lazy to continue this tour.

jokes.text: Bart discovering and playing with the police PA system
jokes.setup: Bored Bart finds police PA system
jokes.punchline: Visual comedy of him testing and playing with amplification
jokes.explanation: Classic Bart mischief setup. Good character integration but fairly predictable escalation.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Whoa, check this out!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Testing, testing, one, two, three...

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Ha! I can hear myself all over town!

jokes.text: I know. I'll go to my room and think about what I did. Oh, no, your room is full of toys. You're going to the, uh, garage.
jokes.setup: Bart offering to punish himself
jokes.punchline: Homer realizing room punishment would be reward, improvising garage
jokes.explanation: Good parent logic. Homer's realization and quick pivot feels authentic to the character.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, I'll go to my room and punish myself.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Your room? With your TV, stereo, and video games? That's a reward! You're grounded in the garage.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: The garage?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, with the spiders and the smell of old paint. That'll teach ya.

jokes.text: For example, there are behavior-modifying drugs. How wedded are you to the Bart you know?
jokes.setup: Wiggum offers parenting advice about Bart's behavior
jokes.punchline: Casually suggests personality-altering drugs with existential question about Bart's identity
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark and philosophical question delivered with casual police authority - highly quotable

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: You know, sometimes a little medication can really help with behavioral problems. Have you considered putting Bart on some personality-altering drugs?

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: I mean, who's to say what Bart's real personality is anyway? Maybe the drugs ARE the real Bart.

jokes.text: How wedded are you to the Bart you know?
jokes.setup: Discussion of behavior-modifying drugs for Bart
jokes.punchline: Doctor casually suggesting personality erasure
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy. The casual way it's phrased makes the horrifying suggestion even funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Well, we could always try a little personality erasure. It's quite painless, and the results are immediate.

jokes.text: It set my brother straight. Now he owns and operates a famous cave
jokes.setup: Wiggum recommending military school
jokes.punchline: His brother's 'success' is owning a cave
jokes.explanation: Peak Wiggum absurdity. The phrasing 'owns and operates a famous cave' is wonderfully ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: You know, my brother went to military school, and look how well he turned out! He owns a cave!

jokes.text: Wow! I can't believe we're going to Disneyland
jokes.setup: Family apparently going on fun trip
jokes.punchline: Sets up the lie about military school vs Disneyland
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the misdirection payoff. Standard but effective structure.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Kids, we're going to Disneyland!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Really, Dad?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Just kidding. You're all going to military school.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Shh. Edna, your tears say more than words ever could.
jokes.setup: Edna is apparently crying about something
jokes.punchline: Skinner's overly romantic interpretation of what are probably tears of frustration
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Skinner's tendency to misread emotional situations

dialogue.speaker: Seymour Skinner
dialogue.line: Oh Edna, your tears of passion only fuel my desire for you.

dialogue.speaker: Edna Krabappel
dialogue.line: These are tears of frustration, Seymour.

dialogue.speaker: Seymour Skinner
dialogue.line: Mmm, yes... frustration.

jokes.text: Military school? You lied to me! Well, I'm sorry if you heard Disneyland... but I distinctly said military school
jokes.setup: Bart's excitement about Disneyland
jokes.punchline: Homer's gaslighting denial of obvious lie
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection payoff. Homer's shameless gaslighting is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, can we go to Disneyland? Please? It'll be so cool!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Disneyland? We went there last week.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: No we didn't! We've never been!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Sure we did. Don't you remember the castle? And Mickey?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, that was a dream you had!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Bart, why would I make that up?

jokes.text: Fortunately, we've had a couple of recent freak-outs... so that freed up a couple of bunks
jokes.setup: Explaining how they have room for Bart mid-semester
jokes.punchline: Casually mentioning student mental breakdowns as room availability
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor. The casual tone about student breakdowns is perfectly unsettling.

dialogue.speaker: Military School Commandant
dialogue.line: We have room for your boy. One of our students had a complete mental breakdown this morning.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh no!

dialogue.speaker: Military School Commandant
dialogue.line: Don't worry, it freed up a bunk.

jokes.text: They're just like the terra-cotta warriors of Xi'an. They sure are. [Groaning]
jokes.setup: Lisa comparing disciplined students to historic statues
jokes.punchline: Students immediately break formation and groan
jokes.explanation: Perfect visual comedy timing. The immediate contradiction of Lisa's observation is expertly executed.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Look at these students - they stand as still and dignified as historic statues.

dialogue.speaker: Students
dialogue.line: Ugh! Grooooan!

dialogue.speaker: Students
dialogue.line: My feet hurt!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: There goes that theory.

jokes.text: But the truth can be harsh and disturbing. How can that be considered beautiful? Oh, he sure sucked the fun out of that poem.
jokes.setup: Student asking probing question about Keats' poetry
jokes.punchline: Lisa's deflated reaction to the analytical deconstruction
jokes.explanation: Good contrast between Lisa's literary enthusiasm and military school's mechanized approach to education

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: But isn't 'Ode on a Grecian Urn' really about the commodification of beauty under late-stage capitalism?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: A thing of beauty is a joy forever.

jokes.text: Oh, he sure sucked the fun out of that poem
jokes.setup: Student giving pretentious poetry analysis
jokes.punchline: Bart's blunt assessment of academic pretension
jokes.explanation: Good Bart voice. Simple but effective critique of overwrought literary analysis.

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: The juxtaposition of the metaphorical imagery with the postmodern deconstructionist subtext creates a dialectical tension that subverts the hegemonic narrative structure.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, it still sucks.

jokes.text: Please don't make me stay, Dad. I'll find religion. I'll be good sometimes
jokes.setup: Bart desperate to avoid military school
jokes.punchline: Increasingly desperate but still characteristically limited promises
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart character moment. 'I'll be good sometimes' shows he can't even commit to being fully good.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Please, don't send me to military school! I promise I'll be good!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Okay, okay, I'll do my homework every night!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: And I won't pull any more pranks on Principal Skinner!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I'll even... I'll even be nice to Lisa!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Well, I'll stop actively trying to kill her.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Look, I'll just promise not to do anything worse than what I usually do, ay caramba!

jokes.text: This school has everything I ever wanted. Lisa, no! This place is just a jail for children
jokes.setup: Lisa excited about military school's structure
jokes.punchline: Bart's horror at her enthusiasm for their prison
jokes.explanation: Great character contrast. Lisa's academic enthusiasm vs Bart's street wisdom.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I think military school could be good for me. The structure, the discipline, the clear hierarchy—

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Lisa, it's a prison. A prison with push-ups.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: A prison with PURPOSE, Bart.

jokes.text: You're a girl? All I want is a chance to prove myself. Hmm. In our 185 years, we have never had a female cadet
jokes.setup: Commandant confused by having a girl applicant
jokes.punchline: Military tradition meets modern gender equality awkwardly
jokes.explanation: Competent social commentary but lacks punch. Functional setup for gender theme.

dialogue.speaker: Commandant
dialogue.line: A girl? In my military academy? This is highly irregular.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Well, times are changing. Women are just as capable as men.

dialogue.speaker: Commandant
dialogue.line: Capable? Yes. But what about our traditions? Our customs? Our all-male bathrooms?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I think we can adapt.

dialogue.speaker: Commandant
dialogue.line: Adapt? Young lady, we've been doing things the same way for 200 years. That's not a bug, that's a feature.

jokes.text: After all, we have female singers... female motorists
jokes.setup: Commandant justifying allowing female cadet
jokes.punchline: Hilariously basic examples of women's roles in society
jokes.explanation: Good period satire. The progression from 'singers' to 'motorists' shows dated thinking amusingly.

dialogue.speaker: Commandant
dialogue.line: We're allowing women into the military academy. After all, women have proven themselves capable in so many fields.

dialogue.speaker: Commandant
dialogue.line: They can be secretaries, nurses, flight attendants...

dialogue.speaker: Commandant
dialogue.line: And of course, mothers.

jokes.text: I wanna quit and come home. Oh, honey, I heard you the first time
jokes.setup: Marge offering support if Lisa wants to quit
jokes.punchline: Lisa immediately taking the offer, Marge's matter-of-fact response
jokes.explanation: Excellent timing and character work. Marge's unflappable response to instant regret is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: If you want to quit, I'll support you.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Really? Okay, I quit.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, I didn't think you'd actually do it.

jokes.text: First of all, Franklin, you are no longer the girliest cadet here. [Feminine] Well, we'll see about that.
jokes.setup: Commander explaining changes due to Lisa's arrival
jokes.punchline: Franklin's competitive feminine response suggesting he won't give up the title easily
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy with unexpected competitive response and perfect vocal delivery

dialogue.speaker: Commandant
dialogue.line: Listen up, cadets. We've made some changes to accommodate our new female recruit.

dialogue.speaker: Franklin
dialogue.line: Well, I didn't join the boy scouts to take a back seat to any girl. I'm still the best cadet here.

jokes.text: First of all, Franklin, you are no longer the girliest cadet here. [Feminine] Well, we'll see about that
jokes.setup: Commandant announcing changes due to female cadet
jokes.punchline: Effeminate male cadet's competitive response
jokes.explanation: Good setup/payoff with character introduction. Franklin's determined response elevates the joke.

dialogue.speaker: Commandant
dialogue.line: Due to the presence of a female cadet, we're making some changes around here.

dialogue.speaker: Commandant
dialogue.line: The barracks will now be segregated by gender.

dialogue.speaker: Effeminate Male Cadet
dialogue.line: Does that mean I get my own barracks?

jokes.text: Company 'L'? But they smell. Yes, we've all heard the chant
jokes.setup: Student complaining about being moved to Company L
jokes.punchline: Commandant's weary acknowledgment of the unit's reputation
jokes.explanation: Good military school humor. The idea of an institutionally smelly company is amusing.

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Sir, I don't want to be in Company L. They're a bunch of knuckleheads.

dialogue.speaker: Commandant
dialogue.line: Son, Company L has always been a bunch of knuckleheads. That's not going to change.

jokes.text: I can't believe they let a girl in. Don't worry. We'll drive her out of the academy. That is why God created hazing.
jokes.setup: Male cadets discussing Lisa's presence at the academy
jokes.punchline: They frame hazing as divinely ordained for excluding women
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about institutional sexism with pseudo-religious justification

dialogue.speaker: Cadet 1
dialogue.line: A girl at the academy? This is an outrage!

dialogue.speaker: Cadet 2
dialogue.line: We need to haze her immediately.

dialogue.speaker: Cadet 1
dialogue.line: It's not hazing, it's God's will.

dialogue.speaker: Cadet 2
dialogue.line: Yeah, the Good Lord specifically said no girls.

jokes.text: Is there any answer I can give that won't result in more push-ups? [Murmuring] No
jokes.setup: Lisa being hazed with push-ups in mud
jokes.punchline: Meta acknowledgment of no-win situation
jokes.explanation: Excellent meta-humor. Lisa's logical approach to illogical hazing creates perfect absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: This is a no-win situation.

dialogue.speaker: Cadets
dialogue.line: That's what makes it fun!

jokes.text: Who's Tim? Apparently Tim was somebody who was with the general... moments before he was shot in the head
jokes.setup: Reading school motto 'I'll die before I surrender, Tim'
jokes.punchline: Dark revelation about the motto's tragic origin
jokes.explanation: Great dark comedy. The casual explanation of the motto's grim backstory is perfectly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: This is the school motto? 'I'll die before I surrender, Tim'?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, what's wrong with that?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Well, it's a bit dark. I wonder what the story behind it is.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Ah yes, it dates back to 1912 when our founder Tim Stonecutter made a last stand against the Springfield Coal Company.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: So Tim actually died?

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Oh yes. Crushed by a mining cart. But he never surrendered!

jokes.text: Get to work! I wanna see my face in that horse's ass!
jokes.setup: Cadets cleaning statue
jokes.punchline: Sergeant's crude double entendre about statue polishing
jokes.explanation: Standard crude humor elevated slightly by military context. Functional but not inspired.

dialogue.speaker: Drill Sergeant
dialogue.line: You better get that statue polished real good, maggots. And I mean REAL good. Make sure you work that shaft nice and hard until it's nice and slick.

dialogue.speaker: Cadet
dialogue.line: Sir, yes sir!

dialogue.speaker: Drill Sergeant
dialogue.line: That's what I like to see - real dedication to polishing. Now get those hands moving on that shaft, people!

jokes.text: Hey, what about me? I'm a lowly maggot
jokes.setup: Bart passing hazing test and being accepted
jokes.punchline: Lisa wanting equal treatment in degrading hazing ritual
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa character moment. Her desire for equal treatment includes equal abuse.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: If Bart gets to go through a humiliating hazing ritual, I want equal treatment!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Lisa, you don't want this.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I demand the same degrading experience as my brother!

jokes.text: Well, since you attended public school... I'm going to assume you're already proficient with small arms
jokes.setup: Starting weapons training
jokes.punchline: Casually assuming public school students know firearms
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark satire about American schools. The casual assumption is perfectly absurd and pointed.

dialogue.speaker: Weapons Instructor
dialogue.line: Alright, everyone grab a rifle. You all know how to use one of these, right?

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Uh, sir, this is a public school.

dialogue.speaker: Weapons Instructor
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, that explains the low test scores.

jokes.text: Four out of five, Simpson. Impressive. But you missed your last target. Did I?
jokes.setup: Bart's shooting performance being evaluated
jokes.punchline: Implied that he hit an unintended target
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection setup. Bart's confident response suggests collateral damage.

dialogue.speaker: Instructor
dialogue.line: You hit the target.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Great!

dialogue.speaker: Instructor
dialogue.line: The wrong one.

jokes.text: Too bad it doesn't run in your family. Um, could someone help me? It's stuck on auto fire.
jokes.setup: Instructor compliments Bart's shooting while insulting Homer's abilities
jokes.punchline: Homer proves the point by immediately having weapons malfunction
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - immediately proves his incompetence after being insulted

dialogue.speaker: Instructor
dialogue.line: Nice shooting, boy. Your father, on the other hand, couldn't hit the broad side of a barn.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? Well watch this!

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.line: Homer raises his gun and fires. The gun immediately explodes in his hands.

jokes.text: If there's a war, just blow on it and I'll come help you
jokes.setup: Lisa struggling with weapons training
jokes.punchline: Condescending whistle solution for military combat
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist military humor. The patronizing whistle system for warfare is amusingly impractical.

dialogue.speaker: Instructor
dialogue.line: Now listen here, Simpson. In real combat, you need to think fast and act faster.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: But sir, I'm not sure I can do this.

dialogue.speaker: Instructor
dialogue.line: That's your problem right there. You're thinking too much. Here, try this instead.

dialogue.speaker: Instructor
dialogue.line: Just whistle Dixie and charge at 'em. Works every time.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Whistle Dixie? That's your military strategy?

dialogue.speaker: Instructor
dialogue.line: Worked for me, kid. *whistles condescendingly* Now get out there and show 'em what you're made of.

jokes.text: Hey, go on, boy. Go get it. Answer the phone. D'oh!
jokes.setup: Homer trying to get Santa's Little Helper to answer phone
jokes.punchline: Frustrated realization that dogs can't operate phones
jokes.explanation: Pure Homer logic. The expectation that the dog could answer phones is perfectly stupid.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Come on, boy! Answer the phone! Answer it!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What's wrong with you? It's ringing!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Stupid dog. He can't even answer a phone.

jokes.text: Well, you're really asking two questions there. The first one takes me back to 1934...
jokes.setup: Lisa asking Grampa if he's busy
jokes.punchline: Grampa launching into completely unrelated historical tangent
jokes.explanation: Perfect Grampa character moment with rambling non-sequitur storytelling

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Grampa, are you busy?

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: Not at all, sweetie. You know, that reminds me of the time I was in World War Two. We were stationed in North Africa, and Private Henderson—no, wait, it was Corporal Henderson—anyway, he had this canteen that looked just like the one you're holding. Well, not exactly like it, but it was metal. And that was during the time when metal was hard to come by because of the war effort. You see, back in my day, we didn't have all these fancy plastic containers. Everything was made of tin or steel. Speaking of steel, did I ever tell you about the time I worked at the steel mill? Must've been '52 or '53...

jokes.text: Well, you're really asking two questions there. The first one takes me back to 1934...
jokes.setup: Bart asking if Grampa is busy
jokes.punchline: Grampa's characteristically rambling non-answer
jokes.explanation: Classic Grampa rambling. Perfect character integration but lacks economy due to length.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Hey Grampa, are you busy?

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: Busy? Well, I was just sitting here thinking about the time I fought a bear for a nickel. Or was it a badger? No, it was definitely a bear. Or maybe it was my brother. He was a mean son of a gun, always stealing my socks. Speaking of which, I can't find my left shoe. I've got three right shoes now. Why do they even make left shoes? Back in my day, we just wore bark tied to our feet with a sturdy vine.

jokes.text: And... that's everything that happened in my life... right up to the time I got this phone call.
jokes.setup: Grampa's long rambling story about his entire life
jokes.punchline: Concludes his life story by bringing it to the present moment of the phone call
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-conclusion to rambling - life story ends with the moment he's telling it

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: And that's how I met your grandmother, and we had your father, and he had you, and here we are now on this very phone call.

jokes.text: Oh, I'm afraid I'd just be repeating myself, honey
jokes.setup: Grampa finishing long rambling story
jokes.punchline: Claiming he'd repeat himself after telling life story
jokes.explanation: Perfect Grampa moment. The irony of fearing repetition after that monologue is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: ...and that's how I lost my shoe in the Korean War. Anyway, where was I? Oh yeah, I'd like to tell you the whole thing again from the beginning.

jokes.text: I've already talked to her for 20 damn minutes. [All] Mm-mmm
jokes.setup: Student complaining about long phone call
jokes.punchline: Other students' sympathetic but judgmental response
jokes.explanation: Serviceable group dynamics humor. The collective judgment is mildly amusing.

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: I was on the phone for three hours last night.

dialogue.speaker: Other Students
dialogue.line: Wow, that's a long time. Who were you talking to?

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: My mom.

dialogue.speaker: Other Students
dialogue.line: Oh. Well... that's nice, I guess.

jokes.text: Bart, who you talking to? Uh- Lisa. You're talking to Lisa. I'm talking to... no one
jokes.setup: Bart caught talking to his sister
jokes.punchline: Social pressure forcing him to deny the conversation
jokes.explanation: Good social pressure dynamics. Bart's forced denial of Lisa shows institutional sexism impact.

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Hey Bart, weren't you just talking to your sister?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Talking to Lisa? Ay caramba, no way, man. I don't even know her.

jokes.text: Then went crazy as a loon
jokes.setup: Lisa trying to find comfort in Emily Dickinson's solitude
jokes.punchline: Remembering the poet's mental breakdown
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor. Lisa's self-awareness about her consolation prize is perfectly timed and economic.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I find solace in Emily Dickinson's reclusive lifestyle. A kindred spirit who understood the beauty of solitude and introspection.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Though I suppose I should remember she had a complete mental breakdown.

jokes.text: But if you quit, it'd be like an expert knot-tier... quitting a knot-tying contest right in the middle of tying a knot
jokes.setup: Bart trying to give Lisa motivational advice
jokes.punchline: Completely irrelevant and nonsensical analogy
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully absurd Bart logic. The complete irrelevance makes it funnier than actual wisdom would.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: You know, Lisa, life's like a burrito, man.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: What? How is that relevant to anything?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Well, if you don't eat it in time, it gets all cold and kinda gross. And that's... uh... that's exactly like your problem.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Bart, that makes absolutely no sense.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Exactly! That's the beauty of it, dude.

jokes.text: Why'd you say that? I don't know. I was just looking at my shoelaces
jokes.setup: Lisa questioning Bart's weird analogy
jokes.punchline: Bart's honest admission about inspiration source
jokes.explanation: Perfect meta-humor. Bart's honesty about his random inspiration process is brilliantly self-aware.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Bart, that analogy doesn't even make sense.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, I just made it up on the spot.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Where do you come up with this stuff?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Mostly from stuff I don't understand.

jokes.text: Like a sock maker working on a top secret sock- Will you stop looking at your feet?
jokes.setup: Bart starting another foot-inspired analogy
jokes.punchline: Lisa's exasperated interruption
jokes.explanation: Great callback escalation. The pattern recognition and interruption create perfect comedy rhythm.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: It's like when you got a foot in the door, right? You gotta keep pushing until—

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Bart, please stop with the foot metaphors. Your grasp of podiatry is as weak as your grasp of basic logic.

jokes.text: My killing teacher says I'm a natural.
jokes.setup: Commandant mentions developing academic and killing skills
jokes.punchline: Cadet casually mentions having a 'killing teacher' and being naturally good at it
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about militarization of children delivered with casual pride

dialogue.speaker: Commandant
dialogue.line: We're developing both academic and killing skills.

dialogue.speaker: Cadet
dialogue.line: I have a killing teacher, and I'm naturally good at it.

jokes.text: That was prior to 1957. Thank you very much, state supreme court.
jokes.setup: Describing old tradition of two-day battle royale between cadets
jokes.punchline: Sarcastically thanks court for stopping obviously dangerous child combat
jokes.explanation: Good institutional satire - military authority resenting reasonable safety regulations

dialogue.speaker: Commandant
dialogue.line: I want to thank the court for stopping this obviously dangerous child combat.

jokes.text: Meet the Eliminator. That's a 150-foot hand-over-hand crawl... across a 60-gauge hemp-jute line with a blister factor of 12
jokes.setup: Introduction of final military test
jokes.punchline: Increasingly specific and intimidating technical specifications
jokes.explanation: Good military jargon satire. 'Blister factor of 12' is particularly absurd technical specification.

dialogue.speaker: Commandant
dialogue.line: Your final test will involve a series of increasingly specific and intimidating technical specifications.

jokes.text: Gentlemen, welcome to flavor country
jokes.setup: Describing dangerous rope course
jokes.punchline: Cigarette advertising slogan for military torture
jokes.explanation: Brilliant cultural reference mashup. Using tobacco marketing for military hazing is perfectly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Commandant
dialogue.line: Welcome to the rope course. One slip and you're dead.

dialogue.speaker: Commandant
dialogue.line: Marlboro: Come to where the flavor is.

jokes.text: This wasn't in the brochure
jokes.setup: Seeing the dangerous Eliminator course
jokes.punchline: Mild complaint about false advertising for torture device
jokes.explanation: Solid understatement humor. The casual complaint about death trap marketing is appropriately absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: This isn't what I expected when I signed up for the Eliminator course!

jokes.text: Uh, hey, Simpson, what are you doing out here? Nothin'. Nothin'? Doesn't look like nothin'. Oh, yeah? Well, um, what are you doing out here? Uh... also nothin'
jokes.setup: Students catching Bart sneaking around
jokes.punchline: Everyone admitting to equally suspicious 'nothing'
jokes.explanation: Good mutual suspicion comedy. The standoff over 'nothing' activities works well.

dialogue.speaker: Student 1
dialogue.line: Bart! What are you doing sneaking around?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Nothing!

dialogue.speaker: Student 2
dialogue.line: Well, I was doing nothing.

dialogue.speaker: Student 3
dialogue.line: I was doing nothing too.

dialogue.speaker: Student 4
dialogue.line: Yeah, nothing here as well.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: See? We're all doing nothing.

jokes.text: Bees. Bees? [Screams]
jokes.setup: Calm night training session
jokes.punchline: Sudden bee attack causing chaos
jokes.explanation: Classic slapstick escalation. Simple but effective physical comedy disruption.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Whoa, check it out Lisa! I'm finally gonna master this skateboard trick!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: That's great, Bart. Just be careful out there.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Relax, what could possibly go wrong?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: BEES! Not the bees!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, what are you—

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Oh no! Run!

jokes.text: Although that's more cursing... than I like to hear from a cadet in peacetime.
jokes.setup: Bart completes rope challenge with mild profanity
jokes.punchline: Commander's measured response suggests wartime cursing is more acceptable
jokes.explanation: Good military logic joke - different standards for cursing in peace vs wartime

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Eat my shorts!

dialogue.speaker: Commandant
dialogue.line: Simpson, in wartime, a man's allowed to curse a little.

jokes.text: Well, at least they're talking to me
jokes.setup: Cadets jeering and insulting Lisa
jokes.punchline: Lisa finding silver lining in hostile attention
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa character moment. Her optimistic reframing of abuse shows both intelligence and naivety.

dialogue.speaker: Cadet 1
dialogue.line: Hey, four-eyes! You think you're better than us?

dialogue.speaker: Cadet 2
dialogue.line: Yeah, with your stupid saxophone and your stupid grades!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Well, at least you're all finally paying attention to me.

jokes.text: Oh, God, I'm delirious
jokes.setup: Lisa hallucinating supportive crowd during rope challenge
jokes.punchline: Self-aware recognition of her mental state
jokes.explanation: Good self-aware humor. Lisa's clinical assessment of her own hallucinations is characteristically analytical.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Come on, Lisa. You can do this. Just don't look down.

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: You can do it, Lisa!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Wait... there's no crowd here. I'm just talking to myself again.

jokes.text: We're gonna make your life a living hell for the rest of the semester. But graduation's in three hours
jokes.setup: Bullies threatening prolonged harassment
jokes.punchline: Timing makes threat comically brief
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection. The timing deflation of the threat is perfectly structured comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Bully 1
dialogue.line: We're gonna keep beating you up every single day for the rest of your life!

dialogue.speaker: Bully 2
dialogue.line: Every day!

dialogue.speaker: Ralph Wiggum
dialogue.line: I'm gonna go live on the moon.

dialogue.speaker: Bully 1
dialogue.line: Okay.

jokes.text: You thought I couldn't, but I could. I did. And I could do it again. Let's do it again! Lisa, it's over. You can put your arms down. I can't. They're stuck.
jokes.setup: Lisa celebrates completing the rope challenge with triumphant speech
jokes.punchline: Physical comedy of her arms being stuck in victory position from exhaustion
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy payoff to Lisa's achievement, undercutting triumph with realistic consequence

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I did it! I completed the rope challenge!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Uh, Lise? Your arms are stuck like that.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: What? No, I'm just... celebrating...

jokes.text: We're gonna make your life a living hell for the rest of the semester. But graduation's in three hours. We better go change.
jokes.setup: Cadets threaten to torment Lisa for the rest of the semester
jokes.punchline: Realize there's no time left for revenge due to immediate graduation
jokes.explanation: Excellent timing joke - villains defeated by basic logistics and time constraints

dialogue.speaker: Cadet 1
dialogue.line: We're going to torment you for the rest of the semester, Simpson!

dialogue.speaker: Cadet 2
dialogue.line: We'll make your life miserable every single day!

dialogue.speaker: Cadet 1
dialogue.line: Wait... we graduate tomorrow.

dialogue.speaker: Cadet 2
dialogue.line: Oh. Never mind then.

jokes.text: They will be fought in space. Or possibly on top of a very tall mountain. In either case, most of the actual fighting will be done by small robots
jokes.setup: Futuristic military prediction speech
jokes.punchline: Increasingly specific and ridiculous warfare scenarios
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist military speculation. The casual 'or possibly' and robot detail make it memorably weird.

dialogue.speaker: Graduation Speaker
dialogue.line: In the future, warfare will become increasingly sophisticated. We'll have laser weapons, drone swarms, and AI-controlled combat systems.

dialogue.speaker: Graduation Speaker
dialogue.line: But it will get more specific. Imagine battles fought entirely in cyberspace, where soldiers are just really angry at each other through their keyboards.

dialogue.speaker: Graduation Speaker
dialogue.line: Eventually, war will be decided by Rock-Paper-Scissors tournaments held on Tuesdays at noon, with a 15-minute lunch break for the generals.

dialogue.speaker: Graduation Speaker
dialogue.line: And finally, conflicts will be resolved by whoever can eat the most mayonnaise sandwiches while standing on one foot and singing the alphabet backwards.

jokes.text: The wars of the future... will not be fought on a battlefield or at sea. They will be fought in space. Or possibly on top of a very tall mountain.
jokes.setup: Serious graduation speech about future warfare
jokes.punchline: Increasingly specific and ridiculous predictions
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist military prediction with escalating specificity

dialogue.speaker: Graduation Speaker
dialogue.line: The future of warfare will be fought not with guns and missiles, but with computers and technology.

dialogue.speaker: Graduation Speaker
dialogue.line: In fact, I predict that by the year 2015, all combat will be conducted via email.

dialogue.speaker: Graduation Speaker
dialogue.line: By 2020, nations will settle their differences through strongly-worded text messages.

dialogue.speaker: Graduation Speaker
dialogue.line: And by 2025, World War III will be decided by a single Instagram caption battle.

jokes.text: Sir, yes, sir! Luckily I am now trained... in six additional forms of unarmed combat, sir!
jokes.setup: Homer asks if Bart returned all the guns
jokes.punchline: Bart's enthusiastic confirmation that he's now more dangerous without weapons
jokes.explanation: Good character development joke - Bart's new skills make him more rather than less dangerous

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Did you return all the guns?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, Dad. Now I'm way more dangerous without 'em.

jokes.text: 'For satisfactory completion of the second grade.' Oh
jokes.setup: Lisa receiving medal for military achievement
jokes.punchline: Medal is actually for basic elementary school completion
jokes.explanation: Perfect deflation joke. Lisa's accomplishment reduced to basic grade completion is beautifully anticlimactic.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I received a medal for my military achievement!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's my girl! What'd you do, invade a small country?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Actually, Dad, it's for basic elementary school completion.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Elementary school? Lisa, that's not military!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I know, Dad. That's the joke.

jokes.text: We thought this time we'd take you to Disneyland for real. [Both] Yea! [Both] Hey!
jokes.setup: Parents offering Disneyland as reward
jokes.punchline: Another fake-out revealed by kids' disappointed reaction
jokes.explanation: Solid callback structure. The repeated pattern with instant recognition creates good payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Kids, if you behave this weekend, we'll take you to Disneyland!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Really? That's awesome!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: We promise to be perfect!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Just kidding! We're going to the DMV instead.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Aw, man!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, that's not fair...

tmdb_overview: Lisa joins the all-male military school where Bart is sent for discipline. Then comes the hazing.
insight: Lisa's military academy arc lands 69 jokes in 23 minutes despite modest impact scores.
